answers.field_label: How Did You Hear About Naiop Other
answers.value:
My first exposure to NAIOP was through a panel discussion hosted jointly by the NAIOP Chicago chapter and the UIC Real Estate Club in fall 2024. The event focused on the future of industrial and last‑mile logistics development on Chicago’s South and West Sides. I attended because I’m interested in adaptive reuse of older warehouse buildings and wanted to hear directly from practitioners doing deals in neighborhoods I’m familiar with.

After that event, my program advisor, Professor Laura Jenkins, followed up with an email to a small group of students who had expressed interest in commercial real estate development. She specifically recommended that we look into NAIOP’s student membership because of the access to local chapter programming, mentorship opportunities, and project tours. She also mentioned that several recent graduates in UIC’s MUPP program had credited their NAIOP involvement with helping them land internships at development firms downtown.

I then went to the NAIOP website to read more about the national organization and the Chicago chapter. I spent a good amount of time looking through the event calendar, the Developing Leaders section, and some of the research reports on industrial absorption and office conversion trends. That research component was particularly appealing to me because I’m working on a studio project that involves repositioning an older industrial corridor near Pilsen into a mixed‑use employment district, and some of NAIOP’s white papers provided data points I could reference in my market analysis.

Finally, at a UIC Real Estate Club meeting later in the semester, a recent alum named Daniel Park, who is now a development associate at a mid‑sized Chicago firm, talked about how his NAIOP student membership helped him build a professional network beyond campus. He described how attending monthly breakfast meetings and walking up to people after panels—something that initially felt awkward—eventually led to a summer internship. Hearing that very specific, practical example of how student membership can translate into real‑world opportunities is ultimately what convinced me to apply for membership this year, rather than waiting until after graduation.
